title: A new approach to bipartite stable matching optimization
abstract: As a common generalization of previously solved optimization problems concerning bipartite stable matchings, we describe a strongly polynomial network flow based algorithm for computing $\ell$ disjoint stable matchings with minimum total cost. The major observation behind the approach is that stable matchings, as edge sets, can be represented as certain cuts of an associated directed graph. This allows us to use results on disjoint cuts directly to answer questions about disjoint stable matchings. We also provide a construction that represents stable matchings as maximum-size antichains in a partially ordered set (poset), which enables us to apply the theorems of Dilworth, Mirsky, Greene and Kleitman directly to stable matchings. Another consequence of these approaches is a min-max formula for the minimum number of stable matchings covering all stable edges.
summary_en: This working paper develops a strongly polynomial algorithm for computing multiple disjoint stable matchings with minimum total cost — a problem generalising several classic results in matching theory. The key technical insight is that stable matchings can be represented as cuts of an associated directed graph, which lets the authors apply existing tools for disjoint cuts. They also show stable matchings correspond to maximum antichains in a poset, opening up the classical Dilworth, Mirsky, Greene, and Kleitman theorems for stable-matching questions. Produces a new min-max formula on the minimum number of stable matchings covering all stable edges.
policy_relevance: Relevant to market-design applications of matching: school choice, medical-resident matching, kidney exchange, refugee resettlement. For Hungarian education-policy design (NKFIH school-admission systems) and healthcare resource allocation: better algorithms for stable matchings directly translate into faster, more robust matching systems. Abstract; specific Hungarian deployment requires engineering work.
